cards.tip: FIX — Ask about HVAC work needed for the remodel project to identify service opportunities.

WIN — Andrew locked in the deal by refusing to bend on ethics (wouldn't share unit-specific info without permission) while solving their real problem—offering recorded-line permission from seller's realtor to get the honest report they needed. That principle + the genuine "good deed" touch converted frustration into trust.

FIX ΓÇö great empathy, but after the reset and vent check drive into the actual diagnosis and put one concrete repair option on the table before you head to the van, instead of deferring everything to a report.
WIN ΓÇö stacking the $5,822 repair against the $8,500 looming compressor and the 42% overpay is what tipped them to a replacement estimate, so keep leading with that value math on every aging unit.

WIN ΓÇö you flipped this by refusing to sink $8,900 of R-22 repair into a 20-year-old system and pivoting to the equipment specialist with a straight 'I'd feel wrong taking your money'; keep leading with that honesty play.
FIX ΓÇö own the first 30 seconds with your name, empathy on the heat, and a one-line game plan, then bridge that solid discovery straight into two or three priced options.

FIX ΓÇö When you inherit an ongoing unresolved issue like a Freon leak nobody can find, ask directly whether they want to keep chasing it or talk options, instead of just topping off and reporting back.

FIX ΓÇö when a customer hands you a symptom like a freezing coil and water backing up, slow down and run real discovery questions before guessing the part, so you can diagnose it and present priced options on-site instead of routing them back to the office.

FIX ΓÇö Your leak diagnosis explanation was excellent, but you never asked for the sale; next time present the $337 flush as a decision to book today before you leave instead of telling them to call the office.
FIX ΓÇö once the room was clearly cooling and the customer was happy, ask for the 5-star review and run a quick health-check instead of filling the wait with small talk.
FIX ΓÇö when the homeowner said the house won't hold temp and feels too hot, dig in with a comfort/airflow diagnostic instead of writing it off as summer heat, and build options around that concern.
FIX ΓÇö once you confirm the bad unit, run the reconnect: one-sentence finding, then two priced options with warranty applied, so an analytical homeowner has something concrete to say yes to.

FIX ΓÇö The customer was actively deferring, but with the insulation and drywall already soaked you had real urgency; lead with a closing question and give at least two options on the $1,285 instead of mirroring her stall.

WIN ΓÇö the doctor's-visit framing and plain-language root-cause teardown built the trust that booked the estimator; keep leading with that on every diagnosis, but next time start the repair-vs-replace options yourself instead of handing the whole pricing conversation off.

FIX ΓÇö after the attic and outdoor inspection, come back and build clear good/better/best options tied to the hot-room finding so the diagnosis turns into a decision instead of ending at the inspection.

FIX ΓÇö after you diagnose the drain pan pitch problem up in the attic, come back down and reconnect with the homeowner to explain the findings and lay out clear repair options instead of leaving them without a path forward.

FIX ΓÇö stop asking the homeowner what they want to do and instead book the free Comfort Advisor on the spot, reframing a new financed system as a selling-point that makes the house move faster so the 'I'm moving' objection becomes the reason to buy.
FIX ΓÇö On a 34-year-old system with a dead pump and no charge, walk back in with a clear repair-vs-replace comparison tied to her same-day comfort need instead of stalling at the diagnosis.

WIN ΓÇö you flipped a home-warranty stall into a signed estimate by arming them with the $5,583-repair vs $7,500-coil numbers and dispatching Derek; keep leading with the customer's goal like that, and next time close it with a review ask.

WIN ΓÇö you turned a 'too much money' wall into a booked Saturday estimate by reframing it as a free, no-commitment visit; next time lock the day and time firm and get the wife on the phone right then so the flip doesn't go soft.

FIX ΓÇö this is a second-opinion shop on a unit we installed, so after you verify the diagnosis come back inside and build clear repair-vs-replace options instead of leaving before the decision conversation even starts.
FIX ΓÇö Get back to the van and present a built option with a real price and a clear recommendation instead of leaving it at 'I'll look into pricing.'

FIX — when a customer signals they're only trying to get a 10-year-old system through the summer, ask a question about their long-term plans to open a replace-vs-repair conversation instead of just agreeing.
FIX ΓÇö after confirming the diagnosis, run a quick discovery and present repair-vs-replace options, using the customer's 'not renewing the warranty' comment as your bridge to flip this into a lead.

FIX ΓÇö you nailed the welcome and discovery, now come back in and build real repair-vs-replace options on that 2003 air handler and flip it to a comfort advisor, since a house being listed for sale is a TGL you can't leave on the table.
FIX ΓÇö when a customer says an old system just needs to last another year or two, treat it as a buying signal and pivot into a repair-versus-replace options talk instead of only reassuring them.

WIN — you built real rapport fast and explained the frozen-coil causes in plain English tied back to their own symptom, so next time lock a firm follow-up on the calendar before you leave to keep that flip from cooling off.

FIX ΓÇö the customer literally asked what you'd charge for the capacitor and connector, so build and present the priced options on the spot instead of deferring, and open a replacement estimate on that failing R22 system.

FIX ΓÇö When the customer leans on their home warranty, don't hand them the job; counter with a question about who the warranty sends and offer a free comfort-advisor visit so two dying 2004 units become our replacement, not theirs.

WIN ΓÇö you flipped a passive inspection into a priced replacement lead by decoding the serials to prove the units were 8 years old, not brand-new, and putting a $2,300 summer-failure number on it, so keep leading with that hard data.
FIX ΓÇö you called the filter 'pretty clogged' but never offered to swap it today; turn that finding into an on-the-spot recommendation instead of just noting it.
FIX ΓÇö on a three-system home, turn the opening walkthrough into real discovery: ask about comfort issues and system age and flag any worn equipment so it becomes a maintenance visit or TGL.

FIX ΓÇö you surfaced $5,300 of compressor risk on both units but let the 'no immediate concern' answer end it; reframe the failure cost plus 8% energy waste and ask to set a comfort-advisor visit to flip it into a lead.
FIX ΓÇö on a 7-to-9 system estate don't leave it as 'call us when you're done'; quote the $300-$400 filters on the spot and lock a booked return date before you walk out.

WIN ΓÇö you closed the $596 repair by proving the fix live and showing the coolant start pumping after a photo-backed walkthrough, which flipped a 'second opinion' into a paid yes; keep leading with that visual proof.
WIN ΓÇö you won this by teaching not pitching: drawing the whole system out on paper built the trust that got the yes, so make that paper diagram your standard move on every install.

WIN â€” Noah met the customer's hesitation (deferring to his out-of-state HVAC son) with empathy plus a direct question about wanting the free estimate, which secured both the signed $1,227 repair and a next-day replacement estimate visit.

FIX ΓÇö after nailing the oil-leak diagnosis and building real trust, don't stop at photos and a second opinion; walk the customer straight into clear repair options and a booked next step so it becomes a lead.

FIX â€” when the customer said he needed to talk to his wife, lock in a specific follow-up date or book the free equipment-specialist visit on the spot instead of leaving it as an open reach-out-in-August plan, which lets a well-informed lead go cold.

WIN â€” earned the decision-ready estimate ask by telling the customer the truth about the rusted coil instead of pushing the quick drain-clean fix, then handling their cost fears with empathy and a clarifying question instead of folding.

WIN â€” He hand-drew the whole system and walked the customer through real pressure and amperage readings, which let the customer talk themselves into asking for the full replacement quote instead of being sold on it.
FIX â€” when the customer said the repair total was more than expected, ask what budget or timeline would work (or offer financing) instead of immediately agreeing and collecting a decline signature.

WIN â€” when the customer tried to shrink the job to just a thermostat, Francisco re-offered the full replacement estimate as a low-pressure price comparison and locked in a Friday appointment instead of letting the visit shrink.
FIX â€” Reframe a selling-the-house cost objection as a home-sale liability instead of agreeing and deferring, then present at least one repair option before leaving.

WIN â€” leading with hands-on show-and-tell of the actual failed parts (rusted coil, leaking motors, hot control board) built the trust that closed a $21.8K two-stage financed system instead of a patch repair.

WIN â€” backed the no-cool diagnosis with hard delta-T and amp readings instead of vague scare talk, then reframed patch-repairs as good-money-after-bad to convert the visit into a same-day equipment specialist replacement lead.
WIN â€” Reinforce anchoring the repair to the identical part that already failed on the twin system, a clean low-friction close that landed a yes despite the customer declining the bigger repairs.

FIX â€” after finding the evaporator leak, rusted coil, and low system score, come back down and walk the homeowner through the findings with repair-vs-replace options instead of leaving the diagnosis in the attic.
FIX â€” When the customer said she'd probably replace the rusted coil eventually, book the comfort advisor on the spot instead of saying it's totally up to her.
FIX â€” cap the personal rapport early and ask the customer what's been going on with the system before walking off to test, so the visit produces real discovery instead of only small talk.

FIX â€” when the customer directly asked what their options were, give a directional price range and value story on the spot instead of deferring everything, so the quote isn't lost to the other two bids already in hand.
FIX — you nerded out great on filters and built real trust, so bridge that into documented findings with clear options before you leave instead of stopping at just the tune-up.

WIN â€” Joe paired the priced $2,735 repair option with the future $7,500 coil-failure risk, which built the case that turned this into a live replacement opportunity for the equipment specialist to close.

WIN â€” used live show-and-tell (ice on the coil, capacitor MFD reading, motor amp draw) to build trust with a skeptical customer and flipped a leak-repair call into a signed, next-morning free comfort-advisor estimate.

FIX â€” After reaching a likely blower-motor diagnosis he should have offered to go up the ladder and put a Sierra repair number on the table instead of deferring the job back to the competitor already on site.
WIN â€” proved the exact fault with a multimeter reading before quoting, turning a customer who refused any other troubleshooting into a $950 damper-motor lead.

WIN â€” hand-drew the full system, showed the debris bucket and failure-photos, and worked every skeptical objection with empathy instead of folding, turning a hold-out into a whole-system comparison estimate request.
WIN â€” drawing the whole system out and giving straight itemized repair numbers instead of a hard sell built enough trust that the customer signed off on a next-day replacement-quote visit.

WIN â€” He drew the system on a whiteboard and showed the coil/refrigerant mismatch and heat-exchanger carbon monoxide risk, which built enough trust that the customer asked for the replacement estimate himself.
WIN â€” He drew out the whole system for the customer to keep and gave honest, transparent price ranges, which built enough trust to get both decision-makers to agree to a specialist visit.

FIX â€” when the customer mentioned he's selling the home, pivot into offering a pre-listing system certification or a maintenance membership instead of closing out a clean tuneup with nothing offered.

WIN â€” Noah answered the sell-the-house price objection with the HVAC operation cost tool, showing added home equity plus 15-year utility savings, then confirmed it landed before moving to a Comfort Advisor visit.

WIN â€” offering a free no-obligation replacement comparison and rescheduling it for when both spouses could be present (instead of pushing a same-day decision) got the follow-up visit locked in for the same evening.

WIN â€” He reframed the replacement estimate as free, no-commitment information and offered same-day scheduling, which melted the customer's scheduling hesitation and got the equipment specialist on-site that visit.

WIN â€” turned a routine maintenance visit into a same-day install-specialist estimate by diagramming the failing parts, tying repair history and a discontinued refrigerant to the $7,500-per-system quote, and calling the office live to send an advisor out immediately.
WIN â€” Reinforce narrating live diagnostic readings and being upfront about warranty exposure, which built enough trust to flip a stalled repair into a same-visit full-replacement estimate.

FIX â€” after building genuine value with the air-loss-to-lifespan math, Andrew should have presented at least one concrete ballpark option and locked a real return-visit date instead of leaving the customer with a story but no choice or scheduled follow-up.

FIX â€” get the actual decision-maker (the homeowner's daughter, who was off-site) on the phone to walk through findings live instead of relying on forwarded photos, since the on-site parent was deferring every decision to her.

FIX â€” when the customer says not today, ask a follow-up question about what's holding her back instead of accepting it and moving to paperwork, especially with a TXV actively flooding and damaging the compressor right now.

WIN — your hand-drawn whole-system walkthrough plus the honest '$5-6K compressor is lighting money on fire' framing built the trust that flipped this to a same-day comfort advisor, so keep drawing it out and dispatching the CA on the spot.
FIX ΓÇö He handed the fan-motor repair straight to the home warranty; next time plant a soft close to book it with us on financing so the job stays on our board instead of walking out the door.

FIX ΓÇö Instead of repeating that we don't deal with warranty companies, acknowledge the claim and pivot: run the full diagnostic and hand them documented pricing they can submit themselves, so the door stays open instead of slamming shut in the entryway.
